cards.back: A variable defined outside of a function. Global variables have file scope and static duration.

cards.text: An identifier with {{c1::file scope}} (also called {{c2::global scope}} or {{c3::global namespace scope}}) can be seen and used from the point of declaration until the end of the file.

cards.front: What value do global variables have if not explicitly initialized?
cards.back: Zero-initialized by default (unlike local variables which are uninitialized).

cards.text: Global variables have {{c1::file scope}} and {{c2::static duration}} by default.

cards.back: Variables with static duration are created when the program starts and destroyed when the program ends.

cards.front: What naming convention is commonly used for global variables?
cards.back: Prefix global variable names with 'g_' or 'g' to distinguish them from local variables (e.g., g_count or gCount).

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: The local variable 'value' shadows (hides) the global variable 'value'. The local variable is used within its scope.

cards.text: To access a shadowed global variable, use the {{c1::scope resolution operator}} with no namespace name: {{c2::::variable_name}}

cards.question: How do you access the global variable 'value' when it's shadowed by a local variable?
cards.explanation: The scope resolution operator with no namespace prefix (::value) accesses the global namespace explicitly.

cards.front: Why should you avoid using global variables when possible?
cards.back: Global variables make programs harder to understand, can be modified from anywhere (making bugs hard to track), and tight coupling reduces modularity and reusability.

cards.explanation:
While non-const global variables should be avoided, global constants (const or constexpr) are generally acceptable because:
  • They cannot be modified, so they don't create unexpected dependencies
  • They help avoid magic numbers
  • They provide a single source of truth for constant values

cards.text: Global variables are {{c1::zero-initialized}} by default, unlike local variables which are {{c2::uninitialized}}.
Prefix global variable names with {{c1::g_}} or {{c2::g}} to distinguish them from local variables (e.g., {{c3::g_count}}).
Variables with {{c1::static duration}} are created when the {{c2::program starts}} and destroyed when the {{c3::program ends}}.
